title: Share of cancer deaths attributed to risk factors
subtitle: The estimated share of deaths from [cancers](#dod:cancer) attributed to all [risk factors](#dod:risk-factor). The remaining share represents deaths that would be expected to have occurred in the absence of these known risk factors.
caption:
Share of cancer deaths attributed to risk factors, World, 2023
The estimated share of deaths from cancers attributed to all risk factors. The remaining share represents deaths that would be expected to have occurred in the absence of these known risk factors.

This horizontal bar chart ranks cancer types by the percentage of global deaths in 2023 attributed to known risk factors, with values ranging from 100% down to 0.9%. Cervical cancer sits at the top with 100% of deaths linked to risk factors, followed by mesothelioma at 91.7% and lung, tracheal and bronchus cancers at 76.4%. Larynx cancer shows 73.4% attribution, while esophageal and colon and rectum cancers both fall in the mid-50% range at 56.4% and 56.2% respectively. Liver cancer registers 48.2%, placing it just below the halfway mark. Moving down the scale, lip and oral cavity cancers show 39.4% attribution, pancreatic cancers 38.7%, and uterine and bladder cancers both at 33%. Breast cancer deaths attributed to risk factors stand at 29.8%, kidney cancers at 27.9%, and nasopharynx cancers at 23.7%. Stomach cancer shows 19% attribution, leukemia 18.7%, and thyroid cancer 11.6%. At the lower end, gallbladder and biliary tract cancers register 11.4%, multiple myeloma 7.6%, non-Hodgkin lymphoma 4.8%, and prostate cancer at 0.9%. The methodology allows for negative values in some cases, which can reflect potential protective effects or indicate uncertainty due to weak or poorly understood relationships between risk factors and health outcomes. The data shows a clear descending pattern from cervical cancer at the maximum to prostate cancer at the minimum, with most cancer types falling between 20% and 60% attribution to known risk factors.
